purpose: Student-friendly virtual instruments that apply the Components test protocol without modifying real chip definitions.
claim_boundary: Virtual instruments can stimulate, observe, estimate, and teach. They do not replace datasheet evidence or physical oscilloscope measurements for hardware signoff.
student_rule: Use virtual instruments to learn what should happen, then use real instruments to prove what did happen.

use_for: forcing active-low and active-high control states
not_for: real switch bounce, current drive, or analog input threshold measurement

use_for: 50 kHz, 1 MHz, 2 MHz, and 5 MHz profiles
repeatable edge tests before physical oscillator tests

not_for: debounce proof unless the physical switch waveform is measured
analog edge quality or ringing measurement

use_for: forced conflict vectors that must fail
not_for: physical current limit or thermal bus-fight safety

use_for: choosing which nets must be scoped
not_for: hardware timing signoff without oscilloscope captures

use_for: checking that chip outputs match truth-table rows
checking that circuit outputs still match after delay/noise injection
making virtual tests fail loudly when expected output is wrong
not_for: physical voltage level, analog edge quality, or oscilloscope signoff

use_for: adding deterministic delay between chip output and chip input
adding seeded jitter or short glitch stress
testing if circuit/system outputs still pass under delay noise
not_for: real wiring signoff without scope captures

use_for: declaring intended logic power in a virtual test
not_for: real supply current, voltage drop, or noise measurement

use_for: declaring intended logic ground in a virtual test
not_for: real ground bounce or return-current measurement

use_for: reset or control-line idle state modeling
not_for: physical RC timing without resistance and capacitance measurement
physical RC timing without resistance and capacitance measurement

student_protocol_steps: Pick the claim: datasheet, model, circuit function, timing estimate, or hardware signoff.
Add virtual instruments that match the claim.
Run the JSON/Python proof and make sure failures fail loudly.
Record what the virtual instruments prove and what they do not prove.
For hardware signoff, repeat the same signal list with real meter/scope measurements.
